message.content:
Try HARD to REFUTE this proposed lever for improving a RACE score. Default to refuted=true when uncertain.

LEVER: Padding cut to fund the synthesis budget
TARGETS: instruction_following (0.25) / Consistent Focus on Restructuring Impact w=0.20; readability P1 'appropriate information density and avoidance of redundancy' w=0.15 (weight 0.25)
CHANGE: Deletion pass over the composed body: (1) metadata-as-content sentences ('published in the Strategic Management Journal', 'received 239 Scopus citations', ALMATO bot counts [36], '525 papers... only 20 papers' [103]); (2) off-topic digressions (AI-lit-review rejection [91][92], Toptal ad content [55], call-center market sizing [99], resume/hiring-process surveys [54] unless reframed as labor-market matching); (3) same-fact-two-sources duplicates (the ChatGPT '2x as likely to report using it' sentence appears verbatim twice, lines 19 and 27, cited to [41] and [61] — catch via fuzzy-match dedup across DIFFERENT citation keys, which is why every existing dedup missed it). Spend the freed ~1,500-2,000 words on the synthesis paragraphs and sector section — this is what keeps total length at reference parity.
CLAIMED GAIN: +0.005 to +0.010 direct; primarily an ENABLER — without it, levers 3-4 either blow past 9k words or cannibalize coverage
CLAIMED FAILURE MODE: Over-aggressive deletion cuts facts the judge counts for breadth, re-opening the comprehensiveness gap; fuzzy dedup at low thresholds merges genuinely distinct findings (e.g. the two different 5.4%-adoption stats in [94] and [95] are the same fact, but the 15% [42] vs 13.8% [71] productivity numbers are different specifications of the same study — keep one, say why). Human-review the deletion list once before automating.

ATTACK IT THROUGH THIS LENS:
BACKFIRE: could this lever LOWER another dimension? Depth levers already cost us readability. Weights: insight .32, comprehensiveness .29, instruction_following .25, readability .14.

Context: our report /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md scored 0.4313. Reference /home/polaris/polaris_project/RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md = 0.5000 by definition. SOTA = 0.5265.
Read the files if you need to check a claim. Be adversarial, concrete, and brief.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
19:The study also documents a 1.2 percentage point decrease in the probability of receiving any employment in a given month—approximately a 10% drop relative to baseline—and a 4.7% reduction in the number of jobs conditional on being employed.[38] By contrast, aggregate labor-market indicators through 2024–2025 show limited economywide disruption; one analysis reports that 35.9% of U.S. workers used generative AI by December 2025 but finds only small positive wage effects and no statistically significant declines in job openings or employment in exposed occupations.[39] A prominent Harvard University study tracking 62 million workers across 285,000 U.S. firms finds that junior positions have been shrinking at companies integrating AI since 2023, with researchers warning that AI is eroding the bottom rungs of career ladders.[40] This finding is consistent with online labor market evidence that entry-level and less-experienced workers in highly exposed occupations bear the brunt of early displacement effects.[38][39] In quantitative terms, treated participants completed the post-treatment task 11 minutes faster—a 0.75 standard deviation reduction—while evaluator grades increased by 0.45 standard deviations.[41] The experiment also documents a reduction in productivity inequality: the correlation between first-task and second-task grades fell from 0.41 in the control group to 0.14 in the treatment group, driven by larger gains for participants who scored lower initially.[41] Workers exposed to ChatGPT were 2x as likely to report using it in their real job two weeks after the experiment and 1.6x as likely two months after.[41] In a large-scale field study, the staggered introduction of a generative AI conversational assistant to 5,172 customer-support agents increased productivity, measured by issues resolved per hour, by 15% on average.[42] Field experiments in online retail similarly document treatment effects ranging from 0% to 16.3% in sales, with smaller and newer sellers as well as less experienced consumers exhibiting disproportionately larger gains.[43] A comprehensive review of the empirical literature finds task-completion time reductions of 15% to more than 50% across writing, customer support, software development, accounting, law, and translation.[39] In software development specifically, GitHub Copilot users complete coding tasks 55.8% faster in controlled settings, with larger gains among less experienced developers.[39] A separate study of nearly 5,000 developers across three field experiments finds a 26.08% increase in weekly task completion, again driven by higher adoption and disproportionately larger gains for junior developers.[39] In a randomized trial with 300 professional translators, a tenfold increase in training compute reduced completion time by 12.3%, improved quality by 0.18 standard deviations, and raised earnings per minute by 16.1%, with lower-skilled translators experiencing gains roughly four times larger than higher-skilled peers.[39] These productivity gains exhibit a consistent pattern of skill compression, whereby less-experienced and lower-skilled workers benefit disproportionately from AI assistance.[39][41][42] Exposure measures across multiple studies converge toward high-wage jobs, with correlations between AI exposure and wages ranging from 0.27 to 0.54.[44] A separate review finds that automating firms show 2% to 10% higher employment and positive elasticities between 0.2% and 2%, suggesting complementarity at the firm level even where aggregate displacement is detectable.[45] The analysis finds that a 1% increase in freelancers' earnings is associated with an additional 0.5% reduction in the number of jobs and a 1.7% reduction in monthly income for text-based services.[38]
27:A joint analysis by the Brookings Institution and the Stanford Digital Economy Lab finds that over 30 percent of U.S. workers could see at least half of their job tasks affected by generative AI, with exposure concentrated in white-collar and entry-level occupations rather than in traditional manufacturing or manual labor.[58] One comprehensive displacement analysis projects that 85 million jobs will be displaced by 2025, while 97 million new roles will simultaneously emerge, representing a net positive job creation of 12 million positions globally.[59] However, the gendered dimension of this restructuring is significant: geographic analysis indicates that 58.87 million women in the U.S. workforce occupy positions highly exposed to AI automation compared to 48.62 million men, highlighting substantial gender disparities in vulnerability.[59] Customer service representatives face the highest immediate risk with an 80 percent automation rate by 2025, followed by data entry clerks with 7.5 million jobs eliminated by 2027 and retail cashiers facing 65 percent automation risk by 2025.[59] Regional variations show North America leading automation adoption at 70 percent by 2025.[59] The OECD Employment Outlook 2023 finds that the share of the employed population with AI skills is small—at most 0.3 percent of those employed in OECD countries on average—but growing rapidly.[60] Researchers find that a one standard deviation increase in exposure to AI is associated with a 0.4 percentage point increase in wage growth, largely driven by occupations that require a high level of familiarity with software.[60] However, men—particularly in finance—were more likely than women to expect a wage increase and less likely to expect a wage decrease due to AI, indicating that AI may put further pressure on currently existing wage inequalities.[60] The OECD case studies of AI implementation find that, so far, AI has led to little wage change.[60] Beyond wages, AI affects job quality through changes in task content and the working environment, with the potential to reduce tedious or dangerous tasks but also to leave workers with a higher-paced work environment.[60] Workers exposed to ChatGPT during the experiment were 2 times as likely to report using it in their real job 2 weeks after the experiment and 1.6 times as likely 2 months after the experiment.[61] The international digital divide compounds these distributional effects, as cross-country differences in occupational structure and digital infrastructure create an asymmetry between advanced and developing economies.[62] In Mali, just 12 percent of today's jobs are exposed to GenAI, but only 10 percent of such workers have internet access, meaning just 1.2 percent of workers in Mali hold jobs that are both exposed to GenAI and can make practical use of it.[62] In contrast, about one out of three workers in Switzerland and Singapore have jobs exposed to GenAI, and all of them have access to the internet.[62] Among all countries in the sample, there are 441.8 million jobs that fall within exposure categories that could leverage GenAI for job augmentation, but 66.9 million, or 15.1 percent, of those jobs do not have internet access.[62] Workers in positions vulnerable to automation typically maintain sufficient internet connectivity to experience displacement effects even in low-income settings, while those who could benefit from GenAI augmentation face substantial digital infrastructure gaps that may prevent them from realizing productivity gains.[62] This finding suggests that developing countries may experience the disruptive effects of GenAI faster than its productivity benefits.[62] There are age disparities in exposure as well, particularly in upper-middle-income countries, where about 10.4 percent of workers aged 16 to 35 face automation risk, while only 4.3 percent of workers aged 56 to 65 are in that position.[62] Among college graduates in low-income countries, 13.2 percent face GenAI automation exposure, and 72 percent of these workers already have internet access, implying that initial displacement effects could concentrate among the narrow segment of skilled and digitally-connected workers.[62] Generative AI also affects labor market matching efficiency, with a field experiment finding that AI-assisted job postings made employers 19 percent more likely to post a job and saved them 44 percent of writing time, but there was no discernible increase in matches formed.[63]

input.command:
cd /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose; python3 - <<'EOF'
import re
def stats(path,name):
    t=open(path).read()
    body=re.split(r'#+\s*References|#+\s*Bibliography', t)[0]
    paras=[p for p in body.split('\n') if p.strip() and not p.lstrip().startswith('#')]
    ws=sorted((len(p.split()) for p in paras), reverse=True)
    print(f"--- {name}: body {sum(ws)}w in {len(ws)} paras; mean {sum(ws)/len(ws):.0f}; top10 {ws[:10]}")
    print(f"    paras>250w: {sum(1 for w in ws if w>250)}   headings: {len(re.findall(r'^#+ ',body,re.M))}")
stats('report.md','RANK10')
stats('/home/polaris/polaris_project/RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md','REFERENCE')
EOF

content.text:
REFUTED. Three of the lever's load-bearing premises fail against the actual artifact.

**1. The padding inventory mostly doesn't exist — budget is 4x overstated.**
In `/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md`: "Scopus" = 0 hits, "239" = 0 hits, "market size" = 0 hits, "Strategic Management Journal" = 1 hit. The lever's named exemplars were audited against a *different/older* report. Summing every sentence that touches the target citations `[36][54][55][91][92][99][103]` gives **11 sentences, 373 words**; add the verified duplicate (63 words, save ~32) and the total recoverable budget is **~405 words, not 1,500–2,000**. It cannot fund a synthesis expansion.

**2. The length-pressure premise is inverted — there is no budget crisis to solve.**
| | body words | paragraphs | mean para | max para | headings |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| rank10 report | **7,652** | **12** | 638 | **984** | 12 |
| RACE reference | **8,721** | **165** | 53 | 165 | 33 |

The 9,194 total is inflated by a 1,558-word reference list. The *body* is already **1,069 words SHORTER than the reference body**. Levers 3–4 have headroom to reference parity without deleting a single sentence. The "enabler" claim — the lever's primary justification — is void.

**3. BACKFIRE, and it lands on the exact rubric line the lever targets.**
Readability P1 is "appropriate information density and avoidance of redundancy." This report's density is **too high, not too low**: 12 paragraphs averaging 638 words, ten of them >250w, one at 984w — versus the reference's 165 paragraphs averaging 53w with *zero* over 250w. Each section is one unbroken stat-dump wall. The lever deletes the narrative/metadata connective tissue (the only low-density sentences present) and reinvests the words in *more* synthesis content inside the same walls. Net effect: **denser, longer walls** → P1 "appropriate information density" moves the wrong way. It buys the redundancy half of P1 and pays for it with the density half.

**Second backfire — comprehensiveness (w=0.29, second-heaviest dimension).**
The deletion list kills sentences that are the sole home of 7 of the 105 cited works. Either you orphan 7 entries in the reference list (an instruction_following/citation-integrity ding) or you drop to 98 works. Commit `fb228dd` shows works-count is a tracked quantity ("8055w, 88 works"). Trading 0.29-weighted breadth for a fraction of 0.14-weighted readability is negative expected value even before the density backfire.

**Salvage (two things, neither is this lever):**
- The cross-citation-key fuzzy dedup is a **real, verified** gap. The same fact is stated verbatim twice — line 19 `"Workers exposed to ChatGPT were 2x as likely to report using it in their real job two weeks after the experiment and 1.6x as likely two months after.[41]"` and line 27 `"Workers exposed to ChatGPT during the experiment were 2 times as likely to report using it in their real job 2 weeks after the experiment and 1.6 times as likely 2 months after the experiment.[61]"`. Different keys, so every key-based dedup missed it. Worth doing — but it is a 32-word fix, not a lever.
- **The actual readability lever is hiding in plain sight: paragraphing.** 12 paragraphs → ~150, plus 12 headings → ~33, matching reference structure. Zero content deleted, zero comprehensiveness risk, zero facts lost, and it attacks P1 "appropriate information density" far harder than any deletion pass can. Reallocate the effort there.

(2) THE LENGTH-PRESSURE PREMISE IS INVERTED — THERE IS NO BUDGET CRISIS. Report total 9,194w is inflated by a 1,558-word reference list. BODY = 7,652w. Reference BODY = 8,721w. Our body is already 1,069 words SHORTER than reference. Levers 3–4 have headroom to reach parity without deleting anything. (3) BACKFIRE ON THE EXACT TARGET (readability P1). Structural comparison:
  rank10:    7,652 body words in 12 paragraphs, mean 638w, max 984w, 10 paras >250w, 12 headings

The report's density is TOO HIGH, not too low. Each section is a single unbroken 600–900-word stat-dump wall. P1 is "appropriate information density AND avoidance of redundancy." The lever deletes the narrative/metadata connective tissue — the only low-density sentences present — and reinvests the words as MORE synthesis content inside the same walls, producing denser and longer walls. It buys the redundancy half of P1 and pays for it with the density half. Net readability effect is plausibly negative.

(4) SECOND BACKFIRE — COMPREHENSIVENESS (w=0.29, second-heaviest dimension). The deletion list removes sentences that are the sole home of 7 of the report's 105 cited works. Either you orphan 7 entries in the 105-item reference list (a citation-integrity / instruction_following ding) or you drop the source count to 98. Commit fb228dd shows works-count is a tracked, judge-visible quantity ("8055w, 88 works"). Trading 0.29-weighted breadth for a fraction of 0.14-weighted readability is negative EV even before the density backfire. The lever's own stated failure mode ("re-opening the comprehensiveness gap") is the modal outcome, not the tail.

Claimed +0.005 to +0.010 is not supportable; expected value is ~0 to negative.
input.salvage:
Two things survive, neither of which is this lever.

(a) THE CROSS-CITATION-KEY FUZZY DEDUP IS A REAL, VERIFIED GAP. The same fact appears verbatim twice under different keys, which is why every key-based dedup missed it:
  line 19: "Workers exposed to ChatGPT were 2x as likely to report using it in their real job two weeks after the experiment and 1.6x as likely two months after.[41]"
  line 27: "Workers exposed to ChatGPT during the experiment were 2 times as likely to report using it in their real job 2 weeks after the experiment and 1.6 times as likely 2 months after the experiment.[61]"
Worth fixing (redundancy half of P1, zero comprehensiveness cost since both keys survive elsewhere: [41] has 5 occurrences, [61] has 2). But it is a 32-word fix, not a lever — it will not move a score digit.

(b) THE ACTUAL READABILITY LEVER IS PARAGRAPHING, NOT DELETION. Split the 12 monolithic paragraphs into ~150 (reference = 165, mean 53w, zero over 250w) and go from 12 headings to ~33. This attacks P1 "appropriate information density" far harder than any deletion pass, deletes zero content, loses zero facts, orphans zero citations, and carries zero comprehensiveness risk. Reallocate the effort from the deletion pass to a structural reflow pass.
